ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for five-spice braised egg. FOODON_03302184 (hen egg food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term for braised/marinated egg preparations is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Eggs from hens) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for braised or five-spice marinated eggs.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 (Eggs, edible) is the closest available concept. No specific SNOMED CT code exists for five-spice braised egg preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One five-spice braised egg weighing approximately 50 g, typically served as a side dish, snack, or accompaniment to noodle soups and rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129); adjusted for soy-braising marinade absorption based on USDA Branded and SR Legacy soy sauce data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled whole egg (NDB 01129 / FDC 173424). Sodium adjusted upward to approximately 250 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce and salt absorption during braising, consistent with published analyses of soy-marinated egg preparations. Five-spice spices contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical usage levels. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are closely aligned with the hard-boiled egg baseline with minor adjustments for marinade uptake.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for five-spice braised egg. GI of 13 is derived from the published GI of hard-boiled egg (GI ≈ 13, reference food glucose = 100) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which draws on University of Sydney GI Database data. Braising in soy sauce and five-spice adds negligible carbohydrate, so the GI of the braised preparation is considered equivalent to hard-boiled egg. GL per 50 g serving calculated as (13 × 0.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium from soy sauce braising marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one egg per serving and be mindful that the soy-based braising liquid contributes approximately 125 mg sodium per egg (50 g), which can accumulate meaningfully within a sodium-restricted diet of 1500–2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Five-spice braised egg has a glycemic index of approximately 13 and provides less than 1 g of available carbohydrate per egg, making it a suitable high-protein, low-GI food choice for individuals managing blood glucose in Type 2 Diabetes; portion control remains advisable due to dietary cholesterol content.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each braised egg contains approximately 187 mg of dietary cholesterol and 125 mg of sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should follow current clinical guidance on egg consumption frequency (typically up to one egg per day within a heart-healthy dietary pattern) and monitor overall sodium intake from the braising marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Five-spice braised egg (五香卤蛋, Wǔxiāng lǔdàn) is a classic Chinese preparation in which hard-boiled eggs are simmered in a fragrant braising liquid composed of soy sauce, sugar, water, and five-spice powder — a blend typically containing star anise, cloves, Chinese cinnamon, Sichuan peppercorn, and fennel seeds. The eggs are first hard-boiled, then peeled and submerged in the simmering brine for 30–60 minutes, allowing the shell-free whites to absorb the dark, savoury-sweet marinade and develop a rich mahogany colour. The result is a firm, deeply flavoured egg with a subtly sweet, anise-forward aroma and a slightly salty, umami-rich taste. The egg white takes on a smooth, silky texture while the yolk remains fully set and dense. Five-spice braised eggs are widely consumed across mainland China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and in Chinese diaspora communities throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore and Malaysia, where they are commonly served as a side dish alongside noodle soups such as Lanzhou beef noodles, as a street snack, or as a protein accompaniment to rice meals. Nutritionally, each egg provides approximately 12.6 g of protein and 10.6 g of fat per 100 g, with a very low carbohydrate content of 1.8 g per 100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 13 (low), making this food suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content from the soy braising liquid (approximately 250 mg per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol (approximately 373 mg per 100 g), which are relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-containing due to soy sauce and is not suitable for vegans.
common_user_queries: What is a five-spice braised egg?

How much protein is in a braised egg?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for five-spice braised egg exists in a primary approved regional source such as HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg data (FDC 173424) with sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy-braising marinade absorption. GI is estimated from the published hard-boiled egg GI value in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled whole egg (FDC 173424) with sodium increased to approximately 250 mg per 100 g to account for soy sauce absorption during braising. This adaptation is consistent with published compositional analyses of soy-marinated egg products. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for whole egg — naturally zero in an unprocessed egg product with no industrial hydrogenation. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — eggs contain no vitamin C naturally. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed zero — eggs contain no dietary fibre. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 173424 and are considered reliable estimates for the braised preparation. 6. GI of 13 is an analogous estimate based on hard-boiled egg GI from Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for five-spice braised egg was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is preserved from the input record as eggs are conventionally grouped with dairy in Singapore food classification contexts. 8. Soy sauce typically contains wheat; therefore is_gluten_free is set to false. 9. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g fields are not present in this record; all micronutrient fields have been populated from USDA SR Legacy data. 10. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology term for five-spice braised egg exists in current releases.
